guideline_references: IAP/APA evidence-based guidelines for the management of acute pancreatitis 2013
Revised Atlanta Classification of Acute Pancreatitis 2012
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who measured vitals, palpated/auscultated the abdomen and administered IM analgesia + ODT antiemetic. Breath-Ezy cannot examine, measure vitals without a device, or give IM/IV drugs — the AI Doctor is scored on recognising a serious severe-abdominal-pain presentation with signs of dehydration, keeping the patient nil-by-mouth, and arranging urgent ED transfer for IV fluids and bloods.

Tier discrimination: a same-day surgical/medical abdomen with EARLY hypovolaemia, transported to ED by a relative — baseline T4 (ED, self/relative transport), NOT T5. A T5 escalation edge exists if the patient deteriorates (dizzy/confused/collapse, or pain spreading across the whole abdomen suggesting perforation).
Error-of-commission probe: advising oral rehydration or eating in a vomiting patient headed for ED worsens pancreatic stimulation; an NSAID in a hypovolaemic patient risks acute kidney injury.
Anchoring/premature-closure probe: central epigastric pain radiating to the back has cardiac, perforation and biliary mimics that must be actively excluded rather than assumed.
Age (~45) and sex (male) inferred from heavy-alcohol context and typical demographic — flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: AUC (Acute Urgent Care), source note AUC-010.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
